questions.q: What are the phases of the cell cycle? Describe the events occurring in each phase. Also, explain the checkpoints present in the cell cycle.
questions.answer: The cell cycle consists of three main phases: Interphase, Mitotic (or M) phase, and Cytokinesis. Interphase is further divided into Gap 1 (G1), Synthesis (S), and Gap 2 (G2) phases. The G1 phase is the first growth phase where the cell grows in size and prepares for DNA replication. In the S phase, DNA replication occurs and the genetic material is duplicated. The G2 phase is the second growth phase where the cell continues to grow in size and prepares for cell division. The M phase is the mitotic phase where the duplicated chromosomes condense and become visible. The sister chromatids are separated, and two new daughter cells are formed. Cytokinesis is the final phase where the cytoplasm divides and two new daughter cells are formed. Checkpoints are present at the G1-S transition, at the end of G2, and at metaphase in the mitotic phase. These checkpoints ensure that the cell cycle proceeds only if the DNA is replicated correctly and the chromosomes are properly aligned.
marking_scheme: Describe the phases of the cell cycle (1 mark)
Explain the checkpoints present in the cell cycle (2 marks)
Mention the events occurring in each phase (2 marks)

questions.q: What is mitosis? Describe the different stages of mitosis, and explain the role of centrioles in the process.
questions.answer: Mitosis is a type of cell division that results in the formation of two daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell. The different stages of mitosis are: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, and Telophase. In Prophase, the chromatin condenses and the centrioles replicate and move to opposite poles of the cell. The nuclear envelope breaks down, and the spindle fibers form. In Metaphase, the chromosomes line up at the center of the cell attached to the spindle fibers. In Anaphase, the sister chromatids are separated, and the chromosomes move to opposite poles of the cell. In Telophase, the nuclear envelope reforms, and the chromosomes uncoil to form chromatin. The role of centrioles is to replicate and move to opposite poles of the cell, forming the spindle fibers that separate the chromosomes during anaphase.
marking_scheme: Describe the different stages of mitosis (2 marks)
Explain the role of centrioles in mitosis (2 marks)
Mention the products of mitosis (1 mark)

questions.q: What is meiosis? Describe the different stages of meiosis I and meiosis II, and explain the difference between the two.
questions.answer: Meiosis is a type of cell division that results in the formation of four non-identical daughter cells with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell. Meiosis I consists of Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I, and Telophase I. In Prophase I, the chromosomes become visible and cross over with each other. The homologous pairs of chromosomes align at the center of the cell. In Metaphase I, the homologous pairs of chromosomes line up at the center of the cell. In Anaphase I, the homologous pairs of chromosomes are separated, and the sister chromatids remain together. In Telophase I, the nuclear envelope reforms, and the chromosomes uncoil to form chromatin. Meiosis II consists of Prophase II, Metaphase II, Anaphase II, and Telophase II. The process is similar to mitosis, but the sister chromatids are separated in Anaphase II. The main difference between meiosis I and meiosis II is that meiosis I reduces the number of chromosomes by half, while meiosis II separates the sister chromatids.
marking_scheme: Describe the different stages of meiosis I (2 marks)
Describe the different stages of meiosis II (2 marks)
Explain the difference between meiosis I and meiosis II (1 mark)

questions.q: What is the role of cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) in the cell cycle? Explain how CDKs are regulated, and describe the different types of CDKs.
questions.answer: Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) play a crucial role in the cell cycle by regulating the progression of the cell cycle. CDKs are a family of proteins that are activated by binding to cyclins, which are proteins that are produced in specific stages of the cell cycle. The CDK-cyclin complex phosphorylates and activates other proteins that drive the cell cycle forward. The CDKs are regulated by the binding of cyclins and the phosphorylation of CDKs by other kinases. The CDKs are also regulated by the expression of cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitors (CKIs), which bind to CDKs and prevent their activation. There are several types of CDKs, including CDK1, CDK2, CDK4, and CDK6. CDK1 is involved in the G2-M transition, CDK2 is involved in the G1-S transition, CDK4 and CDK6 are involved in the G1-S transition.
marking_scheme: Explain the role of CDKs in the cell cycle (2 marks)
Describe the regulation of CDKs (2 marks)
Mention the different types of CDKs (1 mark)

questions.q: What is the purpose of cytokinesis in cell division? Describe the different types of cytokinesis, and explain the mechanisms involved in each type.
questions.answer: Cytokinesis is the process of cytoplasmic division that occurs after the completion of mitosis or meiosis. The purpose of cytokinesis is to divide the cytoplasm and organelles into two daughter cells. There are two types of cytokinesis: mitotic cytokinesis and meiotic cytokinesis. Mitotic cytokinesis occurs in somatic cells and involves the formation of a cleavage furrow, which is a groove that forms in the cell membrane and deepens until the cytoplasm is divided. Meiotic cytokinesis occurs in reproductive cells and involves the formation of a cytoplasmic bridge between the two daughter cells. The mechanisms involved in cytokinesis include the formation of a cleavage furrow, the contraction of actin filaments, and the formation of a ring of myosin filaments.
marking_scheme: Explain the purpose of cytokinesis (1 mark)
Describe the different types of cytokinesis (2 marks)
Explain the mechanisms involved in each type (2 marks)
